original_hypothesis.title: SGX Instance Address Re-Registration via Missing addressRegistered Update in _replaceInstance
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When SgxVerifier.verifyProof replaces an SGX instance via _replaceInstance, the newInstance address is NOT added to the addressRegistered mapping. This allows the same address to be re-registered via registerInstance(), creating a duplicate valid instance ID. An attacker with access to a valid SGX attestation can obtain multiple instance IDs for the same enclave address, bypassing the one-instance-per-address invariant and circumventing the INSTANCE_EXPIRY mechanism by rotating between IDs.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code bug where _addInstances checks addressRegistered but _replaceInstance never sets it for newInstance. Any SGX enclave that has been used for proof verification and rotated keys can re-register the new address, gaining a second valid instance ID.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, ...) but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true; _addInstances at line 195 checks addressRegistered but won't find newInstance

preconditions: SGX instance has been used for at least one proof verification (triggering _replaceInstance)
automata_dcap_attestation is registered and functional in AddressManager
Attacker has a valid attestation for the newInstance address from the replaced instance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _replaceInstance at line 217 writes instances[id] but does NOT write addressRegistered[newInstance] = true
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - the gap exists after every verifyProof call

operational_questions: Can a replaced SGX instance reuse its attestation to call registerInstance for a second ID?
How does having multiple instance IDs affect the expiry bypass?
Is the attestation data reusable across multiple registerInstance calls?
validation_requirements: Trace _replaceInstance to confirm addressRegistered is never updated for newInstance
Test registerInstance with an address that was set via _replaceInstance
Verify that multiple instance IDs with the same address enable expiry circumvention

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: SGX enclave proves a block, replacing oldInstance with newInstance via verifyProof
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls registerInstance with valid attestation for newInstance address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Since addressRegistered[newInstance] is false, registration succeeds and creates a second instance ID
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker now has two valid instance IDs and can rotate between them to bypass expiry
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Access to valid SGX attestation for the newInstance address

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: SGX proof verification integrity compromised; expired keys can remain active
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Invalid proofs could be accepted if the SGX key has been compromised after its intended expiry
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner must manually deleteInstances for duplicate IDs

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single attacker with SGX access
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after first verifyProof call

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple instance IDs mapping to the same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: registerInstance calls for addresses already used in verifyProof
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Instance ID creation spike after proof verifications

validation_result.impact_summary: After verifyProof replaces an SGX instance, the newInstance address is not marked in addressRegistered, allowing it to be re-registered via registerInstance and obtain a duplicate instance ID, bypassing the one-instance-per-address invariant.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _replaceInstance function at SgxVerifier.sol lines 217-221 updates instances[id] with the newInstance address but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. The _addInstances function at line 195 checks `if (addressRegistered[_instances[i]]) revert SGX_ALREADY_ATTESTED()` before registering. Since addressRegistered[newInstance] remains false after replacement, a subsequent call to registerInstance() with a valid attestation for that same newInstance address will pass the check and create a second instance ID. This is a clear code defect where two functions that should maintain consistent state over the addressRegistered mapping do not.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence confirms the defect. _replaceInstance (lines 217-221) writes only to instances[id] and emits an event. It does NOT write to addressRegistered. In contrast, _addInstances (lines 195-213) both checks and sets addressRegistered. The asymmetry is the root cause of the bug.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This function updates instances[id] with newInstance but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. This means the newInstance address can pass the addressRegistered check in _addInstances and be registered again under a new instance ID.

This function checks addressRegistered to prevent duplicate registrations. However, since _replaceInstance never sets addressRegistered for newInstance, this guard is ineffective for addresses introduced via proof verification replacement.

calling_sequence: 1. TaikoL1.proveBlock calls SgxVerifier.verifyProof via IVerifier interface (LibProving.sol)
2. verifyProof parses proof.data to extract id, newInstance, signature (SgxVerifier.sol:133-136)
3. ECDSA.recover recovers oldInstance from the signed hash (SgxVerifier.sol:138-139)
4. _isInstanceValid checks oldInstance is valid and not expired (SgxVerifier.sol:141)
5. _replaceInstance(id, oldInstance, newInstance) writes instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, block.timestamp) but does NOT set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true (SgxVerifier.sol:142, 217-221)
6. Later, attacker calls registerInstance with valid attestation for newInstance address (SgxVerifier.sol:100-113)
7. registerInstance calls _addInstances which checks addressRegistered[newInstance] -- this is false, so registration succeeds (SgxVerifier.sol:195-213)
8. A new instance ID is created for the same newInstance address, violating the one-instance-per-address invariant
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: verifyProof (line 125) -> _replaceInstance (line 142/217) writes instances[id] but not addressRegistered[newInstance]. Subsequently, registerInstance (line 100) -> _addInstances (line 195) checks addressRegistered[newInstance] which is false, allowing duplicate registration. The newInstance address now has two valid instance IDs: the replaced one (id) and a newly created one (nextInstanceId).

deployment_assumptions_checked: automata_dcap_attestation is registered and functional in AddressManager
config_validation.deployment_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol deploys AutomataDcapV3Attestation and registers it: register(rollupAddressManager, "automata_dcap_attestation", address(automateDcapV3Attestation)). This confirms the attestation service is available for registerInstance calls.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Phase 1: An SGX enclave proves a block via TaikoL1.proveBlock, which calls SgxVerifier.verifyProof. The proof contains the old instance's signature and a newInstance address. _replaceInstance updates instances[id] to newInstance but does not set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. Phase 2: The attacker generates a valid DCAP attestation quote for the newInstance address (this is within the enclave's capability since the newInstance key was generated inside the enclave). Phase 3: The attacker calls registerInstance with the attestation. Since addressRegistered[newInstance] is false, _addInstances succeeds and creates a new instance ID (nextInstanceId) for the same address. Phase 4: The attacker now has two valid instance IDs for the same enclave address, enabling potential expiry bypass by rotating between them.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The bug allows the same SGX enclave address to hold multiple valid instance IDs. This violates the one-instance-per-address invariant that the addressRegistered mapping is designed to enforce. The practical impacts include: (1) Bypassing INSTANCE_EXPIRY by creating fresh instance IDs for the same key, (2) An enclave could maintain perpetual validity without genuine key rotation, undermining side-channel protection, (3) Multiple valid IDs for one address complicate instance management and deletion by the rollup_watchdog.
recommended_mitigations: Consider also clearing `addressRegistered[oldInstance]` in `_replaceInstance` if the old address should no longer be considered registered (though the comment suggests keeping it registered is acceptable)
Add an invariant check that all active instance addresses are in the addressRegistered mapping
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy SgxVerifier with a mock AddressManager and mock attestation contract. 2. Owner calls addInstances with address A (instance ID 0). 3. Simulate verifyProof that replaces instance 0: oldInstance=A, newInstance=B. 4. Verify addressRegistered[B] is false. 5. Call registerInstance with a valid attestation for address B. 6. Verify registration succeeds and a new instance ID 1 is created for address B. 7. Confirm both instances[0] and instances[1] have addr=B.
uncertainties: The exploitability depends on the attacker's ability to generate a valid DCAP attestation for the newInstance address. In practice, the SGX enclave that generated the key can produce such an attestation, so this is feasible for an SGX-capable attacker.
The actual impact on proof verification depends on whether having multiple instance IDs provides a meaningful advantage beyond a single valid ID.
additional_testing_needed: Test whether an attestation generated for an address before it was used in _replaceInstance can still be used for registerInstance afterward
Verify that having two instance IDs for the same address actually enables expiry bypass in practice
Check if deleteInstances properly handles the case where the same address exists under multiple IDs
